faqs.question: What is the difference between Class J and Class RK fuses?
faqs.answer: Class J and Class RK fuses have different characteristics: Class J fuses offer space-efficient design at 600V rating with excellent current limitation. Class RK fuses provide dual-element time-delay design for motor circuits with superior motor starting protection. Class RK1 offers highest current limitation, Class RK5 offers lower cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Class J for space-constrained applications. Use Class RK for motor circuits requiring time-delay.

faqs.question: How do I select the right fuse for motor protection?
faqs.answer: Motor fuse selection: 1) Use Class RK or J fuses with adequate time-delay for motor starting; 2) Size at 125-175% of motor full-load current; 3) Consider service factor and starting conditions; 4) Coordinate with motor starter overloads; 5) Verify breaking capacity exceeds available fault current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Size at 125-175% of FLA. Use time-delay fuses for motor circuits.

faqs.question: What is gPV certification for solar fuses?
faqs.answer: gPV (general Purpose Photovoltaic) certification per IEC 60269-7 is required for solar fuses. It indicates the fuse is tested for DC operation up to 1500V and can safely interrupt low overcurrents typical of PV faults. Standard fuses are not suitable for PV applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use gPV-certified fuses for solar/photovoltaic applications.

faqs.question: What breaking capacity do I need?
faqs.answer: Breaking capacity must exceed maximum prospective fault current. Typical requirements: 100kA for standard distribution, 200kA for large industrial systems. Calculate based on transformer kVA and impedance. Add 25% margin. Bussmann offers fuses with breaking capacity up to 300kA.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate fault current and select fuse with adequate margin.

faqs.question: What is the difference between fast-acting and time-delay fuses?
faqs.answer: Fast-acting fuses open quickly on overcurrent, ideal for protecting sensitive electronics and preventing wire damage. Time-delay fuses can withstand temporary overloads (motor starting, inrush) without opening. Use fast-acting for general circuits, time-delay for motor circuits with high starting currents.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use fast-acting for general circuits. Use time-delay for motors and circuits with inrush.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Class J and Class RK fuses?
faqs.answer: Class J fuses are space-efficient with 600V rating and excellent current limitation. Class RK fuses offer dual-element time-delay design for motor circuits with superior starting protection. Class RK1 provides highest current limitation, Class RK5 offers lower cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Class J for space savings. Use Class RK for motor circuits requiring time-delay.

faqs.question: What breaking capacity do I need for industrial panels?
faqs.answer: Breaking capacity must exceed maximum prospective fault current. Calculate based on transformer kVA and impedance. Most industrial facilities need 100kA minimum, with larger systems requiring 200kA or higher. Bussmann offers fuses with breaking capacity up to 300kA.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate fault current and add 25% margin when selecting breaking capacity.

faqs.question: Can Class J fuses replace Class RK fuses?
faqs.answer: Class J fuses can replace Class RK fuses only if the voltage rating is adequate and the fuse block/holder is compatible. Class J fuses are smaller and require Class J mounting. Never substitute a higher ampere rating fuse.
faqs.decisionGuide: Class J can replace RK with compatible holder. Consult electrician for proper replacement.

faqs.question: What fuse class should I use for motor protection?
faqs.answer: Use Class RK fuses for motor circuits. The dual-element time-delay design handles motor starting currents (500-600% of rated current) without nuisance opening. Size at 125-175% of motor full-load current per NEC requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select Class RK for motor circuits. Size at 125-175% of FLA.

faqs.question: How do I ensure selective coordination?
faqs.answer: Selective coordination ensures only the fuse closest to the fault operates. Maintain 2:1 time-current ratio between upstream and downstream devices. Use manufacturer coordination tables for verification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use coordination tables and verify time-current separation.

faqs.question: What size fuse should I use for motor protection?
faqs.answer: Motor fuse sizing: Size at 125-175% of motor full-load current depending on NEC code and motor type. For example: 10HP motor at 230V = 28A FLA, use 35A or 40A fuse (125-140%). Always verify with motor nameplate and NEC requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Size at 125-175% of FLA. Consult NEC for specific requirements.

faqs.question: How does the time-delay feature work?
faqs.answer: The dual-element design has a heat sink element that absorbs energy during temporary overloads like motor starting. This allows 500-600% of rated current for 10-20 seconds without opening. The short-circuit element opens quickly on severe faults.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use time-delay fuses for any circuit with motor starting or transformer inrush.

faqs.question: What is the difference between RK1 and RK5?
faqs.answer: RK1 provides the highest current limitation with superior arc flash reduction. RK5 offers economical protection with good time-delay but lower current limitation. For critical applications, RK1 is preferred.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use RK1 for superior protection. Use RK5 for economical general motor protection.

faqs.question: Can I use this fuse for 480V systems?
faqs.answer: No, the FRN-R-30 is rated for 250V AC. For 480V systems, use FRN-R-30 fuses with 600V rating (FRS-R series) or Class J fuses rated for 600V. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify voltage rating matches your system. Use 600V rated fuses for 480V systems.

faqs.question: What is the typical lifespan of this fuse?
faqs.answer: Under normal conditions, fuses last many years. However, each overload event causes minor aging. Motors with frequent starting may require more frequent fuse inspection. Replace fuses that show signs of heating or damage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Inspect fuses annually. Replace if showing signs of heating or after severe fault events.

categories.name: Semiconductor Fuses
categories.description: Bussmann semiconductor fuses provide ultra-fast protection for power electronics including IGBTs, SCRs, and diodes. These high-speed fuses feature low I2t values, current limitation, and high breaking capacity for protecting expensive semiconductor devices.
categories.longDescription: Bussmann semiconductor fuses are specifically designed to protect power semiconductors including IGBTs, SCRs, and diodes in power conversion applications. As an authorized Bussmann distributor, BeiLuo provides high-speed fuses with ultra-low I2t values essential for semiconductor protection. The 170M series and FWP series offer ratings from 500V to 1500V with breaking capacities up to 200kA. Key features include bolt-on mounting for secure connections, current-limiting designs to reduce fault energy, and fast clearing times under 1ms. Our selection guide helps you achieve proper I2t coordination between fuses and semiconductors.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Semiconductor Fuses
selectionGuide.description: Guide for selecting high-speed fuses for semiconductor protection based on I2t coordination and voltage requirements.

faqs.question: What is I2t coordination for semiconductor protection?
faqs.answer: I2t coordination ensures fuse clearing I2t is less than semiconductor withstand I2t. Calculate fuse clearing I2t from curves at fault current. Semiconductor datasheet specifies withstand I2t. Select fuse with 20% margin below semiconductor rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Ensure fuse I2t < 0.8 x semiconductor withstand I2t.

faqs.question: Why do semiconductors need special fuses?
faqs.answer: Semiconductors are damaged by overcurrent much faster than wires or transformers. Standard fuses open too slowly to protect IGBTs and diodes. Semiconductor fuses clear in under 1ms to prevent thermal damage to expensive devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use high-speed semiconductor fuses for IGBT and diode protection.

faqs.question: How do I calculate I2t values?
faqs.answer: I2t is the integral of current squared over time. Fuse datasheets provide clearing I2t at various voltages. Semiconductor datasheets specify maximum withstand I2t. Select fuse with clearing I2t less than 80% of semiconductor rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use manufacturer coordination tools or consult our FAE for I2t calculations.

faqs.question: What voltage rating do I need for 690V systems?
faqs.answer: For 690V AC systems, select 700V or higher rated fuses. For 690V DC systems, consider 1000V rated fuses to account for DC reversal and transients. Always include voltage margin. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select fuse voltage rating at least 20% above maximum circuit voltage.

faqs.question: Can I use semiconductor fuses for general protection?
faqs.answer: While possible, semiconductor fuses are optimized for fast clearing and may nuisance open on transformer inrush or motor starting. For general circuits, use Class J or RK fuses which are more economical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use semiconductor fuses only for semiconductor protection. Use standard fuses for general circuits.

faqs.question: What voltage is needed for EV battery fuses?
faqs.answer: EV battery fuse voltage depends on system voltage: 500V systems use 500V fuses, 800V systems use 1000V fuses. Always select rating 20% above nominal DC voltage. For 400V systems, use 500V fuses. For 800V systems, use 1000V fuses.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select voltage 20% above nominal system voltage.

faqs.question: What is AEC-Q200 qualification?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q200 is the automotive standard for passive component reliability. It includes tests for temperature cycling, vibration, humidity, and thermal shock. EV fuses must meet AEC-Q200 for automotive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always verify AEC-Q200 qualification for automotive applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate breaking capacity for EV batteries?
faqs.answer: Battery fault current = Battery Voltage / (Battery Internal Resistance + Circuit Resistance). Large EV packs can deliver 20-50kA. Select fuse breaking capacity at least 25% above calculated fault current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate maximum fault current and add 25% margin for breaking capacity selection.

faqs.question: What mounting torque should I use?
faqs.answer: EV fuse bolt terminals typically require 25-30 Nm for M10 bolts and 12-15 Nm for M8 bolts. Use calibrated torque wrench and verify torque after thermal cycling. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use specified torque values. Verify after installation and thermal cycling.

faqs.question: How do EV fuses coordinate with BMS?
faqs.answer: BMS handles moderate overcurrents via contactors. Fuses protect against high-current faults BMS cannot clear. Coordinate fuse time-current curve with BMS trip characteristics for layered protection. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Coordinate fuse with BMS protection curves. Fuse should clear faults BMS cannot handle.

selectionGuide.description: Guide for selecting gPV fuses for photovoltaic applications based on string current and system voltage.

faqs.question: What is gPV certification?
faqs.answer: gPV (general Purpose Photovoltaic) per IEC 60269-7 is required for PV fuses. It indicates testing for DC interruption at PV voltages up to 1500V and ability to clear low overcurrents typical of PV faults. Standard fuses cannot safely interrupt PV circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use gPV-certified fuses for PV applications.

faqs.question: How do I size solar string fuses?
faqs.answer: Per NEC 690.9, size fuses at 1.56 x module Isc (short-circuit current). Example: 10A Isc module needs minimum 15.6A fuse, use 15A or 20A standard rating. This accounts for increased current at low temperatures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Size at 1.56x string Isc. Round up to nearest standard rating.

faqs.question: What voltage rating do I need?
faqs.answer: Match fuse voltage to system maximum voltage including Voc at lowest temperature. For 1000V systems, use 1000V fuses. For 1500V systems, use 1500V fuses. Account for temperature effects on Voc. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate maximum system voltage and select fuse rating above this value.

faqs.question: Can I use standard fuses for solar applications?
faqs.answer: No. Standard AC fuses are not tested for DC interruption at PV voltages and may fail to clear faults safely. Always use gPV-certified fuses specifically designed for photovoltaic applications. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use gPV-certified fuses for solar. Never use standard AC fuses.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 1000V and 1500V fuses?
faqs.answer: 1500V fuses have enhanced insulation and creepage for higher voltages. 1500V systems allow longer strings, reducing cabling costs. Both must be gPV certified. Choose based on your system voltage. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match fuse voltage to system design. Use 1500V for utility-scale systems.

faeReview.content: The PV-20A-1500 is essential for modern high-power solar modules. With modules now exceeding 500W, string currents have increased significantly. The 20A rating handles strings with Isc up to approximately 13A, which is typical for current high-power modules. The 1500V rating supports utility-scale systems with reduced cabling costs. gPV certification is non-negotiable for safety - I've seen too many installations attempt to use standard fuses with dangerous results. For high-power module installations, this fuse provides the current capacity needed while maintaining safety. I recommend this fuse for any installation using modern high-power modules.
faeReview.highlight: High-current gPV protection for modern high-power solar modules

faqs.question: What module sizes require 20A fuses?
faqs.answer: The PV-20A-1500 is suitable for high-power modules with Isc of approximately 11-13A. Modern 500W+ modules typically have Isc in this range. Per NEC 690.9, 1.56 x 13A = 20.3A minimum, making 20A fuse appropriate.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for high-power modules with Isc 11-13A. Verify with module datasheet.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 15A and 20A PV fuses?
faqs.answer: The PV-15A-1500 is rated for 15A continuous current, suitable for standard modules with Isc 8-10A. The PV-20A-1500 handles 20A for high-power modules with Isc 11-13A. Both have the same 1500V rating and gPV certification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select based on module Isc. Use 15A for standard modules, 20A for high-power modules.

faqs.question: Can 20A fuses be used in 1000V systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, the 1500V rating is suitable for 1000V systems with excellent margin. The higher voltage rating also allows for future system upgrades to 1500V without changing fuses. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for 1000V systems with margin for future expansion.

faqs.question: What is the breaking capacity at 1500V?
faqs.answer: The PV-20A-1500 has a breaking capacity of 20kA at 1500V DC. This is adequate for most PV string fault conditions. The gPV certification verifies safe DC interruption at PV voltages. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify breaking capacity is adequate for your system fault current calculations.

faqs.question: Are these fuses suitable for bifacial modules?
faqs.answer: Yes, PV-20A-1500 fuses are suitable for bifacial modules. Bifacial modules may have slightly higher current due to rear-side irradiance. Size fuses based on the higher Isc values typical of bifacial modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for bifacial modules. Size based on higher Isc values from bifacial module datasheets.

With 1500V DC rating and 10A current capacity, this fuse handles standard strings with conventional modules.
The gPV certification ensures reliable DC interruption in photovoltaic applications.
features: Fast-acting protection for standard PV strings

faeReview.author: Jennifer Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Solar Systems
faeReview.content: The PV-10A-1500 is the standard fuse for conventional solar modules. At 10A, it handles strings with Isc up to approximately 6-7A, which is typical for older 250-350W modules. The compact 10x38mm size fits standard PV fuse holders. Even though module power has increased, many existing installations still use standard modules that work well with 10A fuses. The 1500V rating provides future-proofing for system upgrades. gPV certification ensures safety. For standard module installations and legacy systems, this fuse provides cost-effective protection.
faeReview.highlight: Standard gPV protection for conventional solar modules

faqs.question: What module sizes work with 10A fuses?
faqs.answer: The PV-10A-1500 is suitable for standard modules with Isc of approximately 5-7A. This includes most 250-350W modules. Per NEC 690.9, 1.56 x 6.5A = 10.1A minimum, making 10A fuse appropriate for these modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for standard modules with Isc 5-7A. Verify with module datasheet.

faqs.question: What is the physical size of 10A fuses?
faqs.answer: The PV-10A-1500 uses a compact 10x38mm size, smaller than the 10x85mm size used for 15A and 20A fuses. This compact size fits standard 10x38mm PV fuse holders and saves space in combiner boxes. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify fuse holder compatibility. Compact size saves space in combiner boxes.

faqs.question: Can 10A fuses be upgraded to 15A later?
faqs.answer: Upgrading from 10A to 15A fuses requires verifying the fuse holder can accommodate the larger 10x85mm size of 15A fuses. Some holders support multiple sizes, while others are size-specific. Always verify compatibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify fuse holder compatibility before upgrading fuse sizes.

faqs.question: Are 10A fuses suitable for residential solar?
faqs.answer: Yes, 10A fuses are commonly used in residential solar installations with standard 250-350W modules. The 1500V rating provides margin for system expansion, while the 10A capacity handles typical residential string currents.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for residential solar with standard modules. Verify module Isc before selecting.

faqs.question: What is the temperature rating?
faqs.answer: The PV-10A-1500 is rated for -40C to +90C ambient temperature. This wide range accommodates outdoor installations in various climates. Above 70C ambient, current derating may be required. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify operating temperature range for your installation location. Apply derating if needed.

faeReview.author: Jennifer Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Solar Systems
faeReview.content: The PV-30A-1500 is designed for combiner box outputs and inverter inputs where multiple strings are combined. At 30A, it handles the combined current of 2-3 typical strings. The larger 14x51mm size accommodates the higher current rating while maintaining gPV certification. I frequently specify these for the output side of combiner boxes feeding string inverters. The 1500V rating supports high-voltage system designs. For combined string protection and inverter inputs, this fuse provides the current capacity and safety certification required.

faqs.question: Where should 30A fuses be used in solar systems?
faqs.answer: PV-30A-1500 fuses are typically used at combiner box outputs and inverter inputs where multiple strings are combined. They are not typically used for individual string protection due to the high current rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for combiner box outputs and inverter inputs, not individual string protection.

faqs.question: How many strings can be combined for 30A protection?
faqs.answer: The number of strings that can be combined depends on module Isc. Typically 2-3 strings with 10A Isc modules can be combined for 30A protection. Calculate total Isc x 1.56 per NEC 690.9 to verify. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate combined Isc x 1.56. Verify total is within 30A rating.

faqs.question: What is the difference between string and combiner fuses?
faqs.answer: String fuses (10A-20A) protect individual PV strings. Combiner fuses (25A-32A) protect combined circuits at combiner box outputs. Higher current ratings require larger physical fuse sizes. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10A-20A for strings, 25A-32A for combined circuits.

faqs.question: Can 30A fuses be used in 1000V systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, the 1500V rating is suitable for 1000V systems with excellent margin. The higher voltage rating allows for future system upgrades to 1500V without changing fuses. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for 1000V systems with margin for future expansion.

faqs.question: What fuse holder is required for 30A fuses?
faqs.answer: 30A fuses require fuse holders rated for 14x51mm size and 1500V DC. Verify the holder is gPV certified and rated for at least 30A continuous current. Not all 10x38mm holders accommodate 14x51mm fuses.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify fuse holder compatibility for 14x51mm size and 30A rating.
